title: A Conversation with a Beekeeper
summary: An interviewer talks with a working beekeeper about the surprising business reality of keeping hives, and why supermarket honey rarely tells the truth.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Thank you for letting me visit, Tom. I'd love to start with the obvious question — how did you become a beekeeper?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: It was an accident, honestly. My grandfather kept two hives in his back garden, and when he died, the bees were going to be destroyed. I took them rather than let that happen, and twelve years later I have ninety hives.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's quite a jump from two to ninety. Did you always plan to make it a business?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Not at all. For the first three years I just gave honey to friends. I'd never have started selling it if a local café hadn't asked me to supply them. After that, demand grew on its own.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: I think most people imagine beekeeping is a peaceful, romantic job. Is it?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: It's peaceful for about ten minutes a day. The rest is heavy lifting, paperwork, and worrying about whether the bees will survive the winter. A single bad season can wipe out half my income.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: I had no idea. What's the biggest threat to your hives right now?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Honestly, it's a mix. Pesticides used on nearby farms, a parasite called varroa mite, and increasingly unpredictable weather. We had a warm February two years ago that woke the bees too early. They starved before the flowers opened.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That sounds devastating. How much honey do you actually produce in a year?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: On a good year, roughly two tonnes. On a bad year, half that. Compared to industrial honey production, it's nothing — but it's all real, traceable honey from these specific hives.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You said 'real' honey. Is supermarket honey not real?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Much of it isn't, frankly. Studies have shown that a significant portion of cheap supermarket honey is mixed with rice syrup or other sweeteners. The label may say 'pure honey', but the testing tells a different story.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's alarming. How can a consumer tell the difference?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Two simple things. First, look at the price — real honey cannot be produced for two pounds a jar. Second, look at the origin. If the label just says 'blend of EU and non-EU honeys', that means nobody really knows where it came from.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What does your honey actually cost?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Eight pounds for a half-kilo jar. That sounds expensive until you understand that I do every step myself, from inspecting the hives to filtering the honey to printing the labels.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What's the hardest part of the job most people wouldn't guess?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: The emotional side. When a hive dies, especially in winter, you've lost an animal you've known for years. People think of bees as insects, but I think of each hive as a single creature with its own personality.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: I hadn't thought of it that way. Has the job changed how you see the natural world?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Completely. I notice things I would never have noticed before — when the lime trees flower, when the brambles open, when the heather on the moor is ready. I read the landscape now like a calendar.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: If someone wanted to start beekeeping today, what would you tell them?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: I'd tell them to find a local beekeeping association first and shadow someone for a year before buying any equipment. Most new beekeepers give up within two seasons because they bought hives before they understood what they were doing.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Wise advice. Last question — what would you say to someone who wants to support beekeepers but doesn't want to take up the work themselves?

body.speaker: Beekeeper
body.text: Buy honey from someone whose face you can see. Plant flowers that bloom in early spring and late autumn — the bees are starving at those times. And don't cut your lawn every weekend. The dandelions you remove are the first food many bees see in the year.

annotations.exampleEn: He keeps twelve hives at the bottom of his garden.
We supply three local restaurants every week.
Renovating an old house involves a lot of heavy lifting.
A bad winter can wipe out half my hives.
Pesticides used on nearby farms can drift into the hives.

annotations.meaningVi: ve varroa (ký sinh trùng tổ ong)
annotations.exampleEn: The varroa mite is the single biggest killer of European honeybees.
The bees starved before the flowers opened.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể truy xuất nguồn gốc
annotations.exampleEn: All our honey is traceable to a single apiary.
Cheap honey is often mixed with rice syrup to cut costs.

annotations.meaningVi: kể một câu chuyện khác hẳn (sự thật khác với bề ngoài)
annotations.exampleEn: The label says 'organic', but the testing tells a different story.

annotations.phrase: blend of EU and non-EU honeys
annotations.meaningVi: hỗn hợp mật ong EU và ngoài EU (nguồn gốc mơ hồ)
annotations.pronunciation: /blend əv iː juː ən nɒn iː juː ˈhʌniz/
annotations.exampleEn: If the label says 'blend of EU and non-EU honeys', the origin is essentially unknown.

annotations.meaningVi: đi theo học việc, quan sát ai đó làm việc
annotations.exampleEn: Shadow an experienced nurse for a few weeks before you decide.

annotations.meaningVi: bỏ cuộc trong vòng hai mùa
annotations.exampleEn: Most new beekeepers give up within two seasons.

annotations.meaningVi: đang đói trong những lúc đó
annotations.exampleEn: The bees are starving at those times, so early flowers matter.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional for Counterfactual History
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người phỏng vấn dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 để nói về điều đã không xảy ra: 'I'd never have started selling it if a local café hadn't asked me to supply them' (tôi đã không bắt đầu nếu quán cà phê đã không yêu cầu — nhưng quán cà phê đã yêu cầu). Cấu trúc: 'would/wouldn't have + V3 + if + had/hadn't + V3'. Dùng khi muốn suy nghĩ về một quá khứ giả định khác với thực tế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The beekeeper uses third conditional to talk about what did not happen: 'I'd never have started selling it if a local café hadn't asked me to supply them.' Structure: 'would/wouldn't have + past participle + if + had/hadn't + past participle'. Use when reflecting on a counterfactual past — what would have happened if reality had been different.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech and Hedged Assertions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại minh hoạ cách dùng tường thuật gián tiếp và lối nói giảm để thông báo thông tin: 'Studies have shown that a significant portion of cheap supermarket honey is mixed with rice syrup', 'The label may say "pure honey", but the testing tells a different story', 'I'd tell them to find a local beekeeping association first'. Khi truyền đạt thông tin từ nghiên cứu hoặc khi đưa lời khuyên, dùng các cấu trúc như 'studies have shown', 'I would tell them to', 'may + verb' để nghe khách quan và lịch sự hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue illustrates reported speech and hedged assertions: 'Studies have shown that...', 'The label may say...', 'I'd tell them to find a local beekeeping association first'. When relaying information from research or giving advice, structures like 'studies have shown', 'I would tell them to', and 'may + verb' sound more objective and polite.

translationVi:
Người phỏng vấn: Cảm ơn Tom đã cho tôi đến thăm. Tôi muốn bắt đầu bằng câu hỏi hiển nhiên — anh trở thành người nuôi ong bằng cách nào?

Người nuôi ong: Đó là tình cờ, thật đấy. Ông tôi nuôi hai tổ ong sau vườn, và khi ông mất, đàn ong sắp bị tiêu hủy. Tôi đã nhận lấy thay vì để chuyện đó xảy ra, và mười hai năm sau tôi có chín mươi tổ.

Người phỏng vấn: Đó là bước nhảy khá lớn từ hai lên chín mươi. Anh có luôn dự định biến nó thành công việc kinh doanh không?

Người nuôi ong: Không hề. Ba năm đầu tôi chỉ cho bạn bè mật ong. Tôi đã không bao giờ bắt đầu bán nếu một quán cà phê địa phương không yêu cầu tôi cung cấp. Sau đó, nhu cầu tự tăng lên.

Người phỏng vấn: Tôi nghĩ hầu hết mọi người tưởng tượng nuôi ong là một công việc lãng mạn, yên bình. Có phải vậy không?

Người nuôi ong: Nó yên bình khoảng mười phút mỗi ngày. Phần còn lại là công việc nặng nhọc, giấy tờ, và lo lắng xem đàn ong có sống qua mùa đông không. Một mùa tồi có thể xoá sạch một nửa thu nhập của tôi.

Người phỏng vấn: Tôi không hề biết. Mối đe doạ lớn nhất với tổ ong của anh hiện nay là gì?

Người nuôi ong: Thật ra là hỗn hợp. Thuốc trừ sâu dùng ở các trang trại lân cận, một loại ký sinh trùng gọi là ve varroa, và thời tiết ngày càng khó đoán. Hai năm trước chúng tôi có một tháng Hai ấm áp đánh thức ong quá sớm. Chúng chết đói trước khi hoa nở.

Người phỏng vấn: Nghe thật đau lòng. Mỗi năm anh sản xuất được bao nhiêu mật ong?

Người nuôi ong: Năm tốt, khoảng hai tấn. Năm tồi, một nửa số đó. So với sản xuất công nghiệp thì chẳng là gì — nhưng đều là mật ong thật, có thể truy xuất nguồn gốc từ chính các tổ này.

Người phỏng vấn: Anh vừa nói 'thật'. Mật ong siêu thị không thật à?

Người nuôi ong: Phần lớn là không, thẳng thắn mà nói. Các nghiên cứu cho thấy một phần đáng kể mật ong siêu thị giá rẻ được trộn với sirô gạo hoặc các chất ngọt khác. Nhãn có thể ghi 'mật ong nguyên chất', nhưng kết quả kiểm nghiệm kể một câu chuyện khác.

Người phỏng vấn: Thật đáng báo động. Người tiêu dùng làm sao phân biệt được?

Người nuôi ong: Hai điều đơn giản. Thứ nhất, nhìn vào giá — mật ong thật không thể sản xuất với giá hai bảng một lọ. Thứ hai, nhìn vào xuất xứ. Nếu nhãn chỉ ghi 'hỗn hợp mật ong EU và ngoài EU', nghĩa là không ai thực sự biết nó từ đâu đến.

Người phỏng vấn: Mật ong của anh giá bao nhiêu?

Người nuôi ong: Tám bảng cho lọ nửa cân. Nghe đắt cho đến khi bạn hiểu rằng tôi tự làm mọi bước, từ kiểm tra tổ, lọc mật, đến in nhãn.

Người phỏng vấn: Phần khó nhất của công việc mà hầu hết mọi người không đoán ra là gì?

Người nuôi ong: Phần cảm xúc. Khi một tổ chết, đặc biệt vào mùa đông, bạn đã mất một sinh vật bạn đã quen biết nhiều năm. Người ta nghĩ ong là côn trùng, nhưng tôi nghĩ mỗi tổ là một sinh vật duy nhất với cá tính riêng.

Người phỏng vấn: Tôi chưa từng nghĩ theo cách đó. Công việc có thay đổi cách anh nhìn thế giới tự nhiên không?

Người nuôi ong: Hoàn toàn. Tôi nhận ra những điều trước đây tôi sẽ không bao giờ nhận ra — khi cây bồ đề nở hoa, khi bụi mâm xôi mở ra, khi cây thạch nam trên đồng hoang sẵn sàng. Bây giờ tôi đọc cảnh quan như đọc cuốn lịch.

Người phỏng vấn: Nếu ai đó muốn bắt đầu nuôi ong hôm nay, anh sẽ khuyên gì?

Người nuôi ong: Tôi sẽ bảo họ trước tiên tìm một hội nuôi ong địa phương và đi theo học việc với ai đó một năm trước khi mua bất kỳ thiết bị nào. Hầu hết người mới nuôi ong bỏ cuộc trong vòng hai mùa vì họ mua tổ ong trước khi hiểu họ đang làm gì.

Người phỏng vấn: Lời khuyên khôn ngoan. Câu hỏi cuối — anh sẽ nói gì với người muốn ủng hộ người nuôi ong nhưng không muốn tự làm?

Người nuôi ong: Mua mật ong từ người mà bạn thấy được khuôn mặt. Trồng hoa nở vào đầu xuân và cuối thu — ong đang đói vào những thời điểm đó. Và đừng cắt cỏ mỗi cuối tuần. Hoa bồ công anh bạn nhổ chính là thức ăn đầu tiên nhiều con ong nhìn thấy trong năm.

questions.prompt: How did Tom become a beekeeper?
options: He converted from another type of farming
questions.explanation: Tom says his grandfather kept hives, and 'when he died, the bees were going to be destroyed. I took them rather than let that happen.'
questions.hint: It started as a rescue, not a plan.

questions.prompt: How many hives does Tom now keep?
questions.explanation: Tom says 'twelve years later I have ninety hives.'
questions.hint: He started with two — listen for the current number.

questions.prompt: What made Tom start selling honey?
options: A bank loan he had to repay
A local café asked him to supply them
His friends complained about free honey
questions.explanation: Tom says 'I'd never have started selling it if a local café hadn't asked me to supply them.'
questions.hint: It was an external request, not his own plan.

questions.prompt: How peaceful is beekeeping, according to Tom?
options: Peaceful for about ten minutes a day
questions.explanation: Tom says 'It's peaceful for about ten minutes a day.'
questions.hint: Tom gives a very specific small amount of time.

questions.prompt: What are the three threats Tom identifies to his hives?
options: Pesticides, the varroa mite, and unpredictable weather
questions.explanation: Tom says 'Pesticides used on nearby farms, a parasite called varroa mite, and increasingly unpredictable weather.'

questions.prompt: What happened in February two years ago?
options: An unusually warm February woke the bees too early and they starved
questions.explanation: Tom says 'a warm February two years ago that woke the bees too early. They starved before the flowers opened.'

questions.prompt: How much honey does Tom produce in a good year?
questions.explanation: Tom says 'On a good year, roughly two tonnes.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific quantity for a good year.

questions.prompt: What is Tom's claim about cheap supermarket honey?
options: It is healthier than artisan honey
Studies have shown a significant portion is mixed with rice syrup or other sweeteners
It is always imported from one specific country
It contains too much sugar from natural sources
questions.explanation: Tom says 'Studies have shown that a significant portion of cheap supermarket honey is mixed with rice syrup or other sweeteners.'
questions.hint: His claim is supported by studies, not just opinion.

questions.prompt: What two checks does Tom recommend a consumer make?
options: The price and the origin label
The country and the brand name
The bottle shape and the seller's reputation
questions.explanation: Tom says 'First, look at the price — real honey cannot be produced for two pounds a jar. Second, look at the origin.'
questions.hint: Two specific things on the label and price tag.

questions.prompt: How does Tom describe each hive emotionally?
options: As a collection of individual insects
As a single creature with its own personality
questions.explanation: Tom says 'I think of each hive as a single creature with its own personality.'
questions.hint: Tom challenges the idea of bees as merely insects.

questions.prompt: How has beekeeping changed Tom's view of the natural world?
options: He notices nature less because he is busy
He reads the landscape like a calendar, noticing flowering times
He has become afraid of insects
He has lost interest in plants
questions.explanation: Tom says 'I notice things I would never have noticed before... I read the landscape now like a calendar.'
questions.hint: His attention has shifted to the seasonal timing of plants.

questions.prompt: What is Tom's first advice to someone wanting to start beekeeping?
options: Buy two hives immediately to learn
Find a local beekeeping association and shadow someone for a year before buying equipment
questions.explanation: Tom says 'find a local beekeeping association first and shadow someone for a year before buying any equipment.'
questions.hint: His advice is to learn before spending.

questions.prompt: Across the interview, what is Tom's main message about beekeeping?
options: It is easier than people think and a great career change
It is emotionally and financially demanding, and quality honey costs what it costs for good reasons
It will disappear within twenty years
Anyone who loves nature should try it
questions.explanation: Across the dialogue Tom emphasises heavy lifting, hive losses, the difference between real and adulterated honey, and the eight-pound price reflecting real work.
questions.hint: Synthesise the threads about workload, losses, and the economics of real honey.

cloze.template:
Interviewer: How did you become a beekeeper?
Beekeeper: It was an {{b1}}, honestly. My grandfather kept two hives, and when he died, the bees were going to be destroyed. I took them rather than let that happen, and twelve years later I have ninety hives.
Interviewer: Did you always plan to make it a business?
Beekeeper: Not at all. I'd never have started selling it if a local café hadn't asked me to {{b2}} them.
Interviewer: Is it a peaceful job?
Beekeeper: It's peaceful for about ten minutes a day. The rest is heavy {{b3}}, paperwork, and worrying about whether the bees will survive the winter.
Interviewer: What's the biggest threat to your hives?
Beekeeper: Pesticides used on nearby farms, a parasite called varroa {{b4}}, and unpredictable weather. A warm February two years ago woke the bees too early. They {{b5}} before the flowers opened.
Interviewer: Is supermarket honey real?
Beekeeper: Studies have shown that a significant portion of cheap supermarket honey is mixed with rice {{b6}}. The label may say 'pure honey', but the testing tells a different {{b7}}.
Interviewer: How can a consumer tell the difference?
Beekeeper: Look at the price — real honey cannot be produced for two pounds a jar. And look at the {{b8}}.
Interviewer: What's the hardest part of the job?
Beekeeper: The emotional side. When a hive dies, you've lost an animal you've known for years. I think of each hive as a single creature with its own {{b9}}.
Interviewer: What would you tell a beginner?
Beekeeper: Find a local beekeeping association and {{b10}} someone for a year before buying any equipment.

blanks.explanation: Tom says 'It was an accident, honestly' — he didn't plan to become a beekeeper.
'Supply' is the exact verb used — to be a regular supplier to a business.
'Heavy lifting' is the fixed expression for physically demanding work.
'Varroa mite' is the specific scientific name of the parasite — Tom uses the exact term.
Tom says the bees 'starved before the flowers opened' — they died of hunger specifically.
'Rice syrup' is the exact term Tom uses for the cheap sweetener.
'Tells a different story' is the fixed idiom — meaning the truth is different from the appearance.
Tom says 'look at the origin' — the source country information on the label.
Tom says 'a single creature with its own personality' — the exact word he uses.
'Shadow' specifically means to follow and observe someone at work to learn — Tom's exact verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tom says he reads the landscape like a calendar now. How would your daily life change if your livelihood depended on noticing exactly when plants flower? Is there value in learning to pay that kind of attention even if your job doesn't require it?
